planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; rocket ligularia wilts dramatically in full sun and afternoon heat, signaling water stress even when soil is moist — afternoon shade is essential in zones 6–8.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; rocket ligularia (the rocket) is one of the most dramatic and architecturally impressive shade garden perennials available — the cultivar 'The Rocket' produces enormous, rounded-to-triangular, deeply veined, dark-green leaves on tall, dark-mahogany stems, and in midsummer erupts into flowering with tall, bold, upright spires of small, sharp-yellow, daisy-like flowers packed densely along 3–4-foot stems that tower dramatically above the foliage mound. The combination of bold, tropical-scale foliage and tall, vivid-yellow flower spires creates an unforgettable effect in the moist shade garden. Ligularia is notorious for wilting dramatically in warm afternoon sun even when soil is moist (a physiological response to transpiration demand exceeding root uptake), making afternoon shade essential; plants look perfectly healthy in the morning and collapsed by afternoon. This is a normal behavior and plants recover when temperatures drop in the evening. Excellent beside ponds, streams, and rain garden margins. 'The Rocket' (Japan), 'Britt-Marie Crawford' (very dark, near-black foliage), and 'Little Rocket' (compact) are key cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; giant fleece flower is a substantial plant requiring considerable space — allow 5–6 feet on all sides and site away from delicate smaller companions.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; giant fleece flower is a large, boldly impressive East Asian perennial that produces, from spring through autumn, an enormous vase-shaped clump of large, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves rising to 5–7 feet in height, crowned from late June through August by enormous, loose, feathery, billowing clouds of tiny, creamy-white flowers in large branching panicles that create an airy, frothy, white-mist effect on a massive scale — one of the most impressive flower mass displays possible in the perennial garden. Unlike the weedy Japanese knotweed (Reynoutria japonica), giant fleece flower behaves as a well-mannered, clumping, non-running perennial that does not spread beyond its designated space and requires no containment. The flower heads age through pinkish-buff to tan as they dry in place, extending the display through late summer and into fall. Cut the entire plant to the ground in late winter for the cleanest regrowth. An excellent background specimen for large herbaceous borders, beside water features, and in naturalistic settings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; mountain fleece is exceptionally adaptable and performs in a wide range of conditions from full sun to quite deep partial shade.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to moderately dry, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; mountain fleece (red bistort) is a Himalayan perennial that has become one of the most celebrated and widely grown border perennials in European gardens for its extraordinary five-month flowering period — from late June through November it produces an uninterrupted succession of small, upright, bottle-brush-like flower spikes of rich, deep crimson-red, rosy-red, or white on compact, well-foliaged plants, outperforming most border perennials in duration of bloom by a considerable margin. The plant forms a well-behaved, non-spreading clump of large, heart-shaped leaves that provide an attractive foliage mass independent of the flowers. It tolerates partial shade well, maintaining its blooming performance in dappled light where many full-sun perennials would fail to flower. 'Firetail' (deep crimson-red), 'Golden Arrow' (gold-variegated leaves), 'Album' (white), and 'Inverleith' (compact, dark red) are widely grown cultivars. The flower spikes are attractive to bumblebees throughout the season.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade beside ponds, streams, and in rain gardens in spring; umbrella plant thrives in shallow water (up to 6 inches deep) and wet boggy soil and requires consistently moist conditions throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade beside water; umbrella plant (Indian rhubarb) is a native Californian and Oregon perennial of streambanks and wet canyon floors with two distinct ornamental seasons: in early spring, before any leaves emerge, clusters of small, flat-topped, pale-pink flowers appear on 3-foot, bare, hairy reddish stems — a surprising and charming early display; then throughout summer and fall, enormous, round, peltate (stalked from the center) leaves up to 2 feet across rise on tall, thick, hairy petioles to 3–4 feet, creating a bold, parasol-like effect that is one of the most architecturally dramatic of any streamside or bog perennial. The leaves turn to rich coppery-orange in autumn before dying back. An excellent specimen plant for large-scale naturalistic plantings beside ponds and streams where its scale and requirement for constantly wet soil can be accommodated. Similar in scale to Petasites (butterbur) but native to western North America and much better-behaved (non-spreading).

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; shredded umbrella plant is a slow-establishing but very long-lived perennial — patience through the first two seasons is rewarded with permanent, essentially maintenance-free shade garden coverage thereafter.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; shredded umbrella plant is one of the most unusual and conversation-inspiring perennials in the shade garden — each spring the new leaves emerge in a remarkable juvenile form, looking like a closed, tattered umbrella with deeply divided, drooping, shaggy segments still folded together; as the leaves expand and unfurl, they reveal mature, deeply palmate, aconitum-like leaves with elegant, fingerlike divisions held flat in an umbrella shape. The effect of the emerging juvenile growth is bizarre and fascinating. Mature plants bear small, inconspicuous, white daisy flowers in late summer. The plant is native to shaded rocky hillsides and open woodlands in Korea, China, and Japan and is completely winter-hardy while tolerating heat better than many shade perennials. An excellent foliage specimen for collectors, shade garden enthusiasts, and gardeners who appreciate unusual plant texture and form over flashy flowers. Slow to establish from division; patience is required.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
